title: Outdoor Modern Bar Table with X Base
summary: A bar-height slat-top table on four straight legs with a single 45°-mitered X cross brace — the brace can skip glue if you want it to knock down.
description:
This table's identity is the X cross brace between the legs — one long diagonal mitered at 45° on both ends, meeting two shorter diagonal pieces mitered on one end each. Get the long brace's angles right and the two short pieces just need to meet it cleanly; dry-fit all three before committing to final fasteners.

The plan deliberately leaves glue out of the cross-brace joint as an option: skip it there and the table can be knocked back down for storage or moving, while every other joint stays permanently fastened with screws and glue as usual. That's a genuinely useful trick worth remembering for any table you might need to break down seasonally.

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 45°, parallel to each other.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two side aprons at 32" and four end aprons and supports at 30-1/2" from the 1x3. Cut nine tabletop boards at 33-1/2", four legs at 41-1/4", one long cross support at 46" and two short cross supports at 22-3/4" from the 1x4. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to two 8 ft 1x3 and six 10 ft 1x4. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the tabletop frame
steps.body: Build the tabletop frame from the side and end aprons using 2" screws and glue. Check for square before moving on.

steps.title: Attach the tabletop boards
steps.body: Screw the tabletop boards down across the frame, spaced evenly. A pocket-hole jig lets you drive screws from the frame up into the underside of the tabletop boards so no screw heads show on top.

steps.title: Add the legs
steps.body: Attach the four legs to the underside of the tabletop frame at the corners.

steps.title: Add the cross brace
steps.body: Dry-fit the long and two short cross-support pieces between the legs to confirm the mitered ends meet cleanly, then fasten. Skip glue on this joint if you'd like the option to knock the table down for storage later — every other joint in the table should still get glue as usual.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: If prefinishing, fill any holes with wood filler or paintable silicone, sand as needed, and apply a finish coat of exterior paint.

images.alt: Outdoor bar-height table with a slat top and an X-shaped cross brace
